Riley says, "That means the plan helped auto-insurance policyholders." Which hidden assumption does Riley's argument need?

choices.text: Reported minor crashes can never be measured reliably.
Measuring reported minor crashes is a reasonable way to judge whether the plan helped auto-insurance policyholders.
The insurer should reject every alternative to the idea.
Auto-insurance policyholders caused the test result by themselves.

explanation: The hidden assumption is that the measured result is a good sign of whether the plan helped.

prompt: In a memo about evening bus service for late-shift workers, Riley writes, "During a small trial of extending evening bus service until midnight, late-shift commute complaints fell by 22% compared with the four weeks before the test. So the action caused the change." What is the logical gap in Riley's reasoning?

choices.text: It assumes timing alone proves causation.
It uses a source with no possible incentive.
It includes a specific number instead of a story.
It states the conclusion before the evidence.

explanation: The logical gap is treating timing as proof of cause. Something happening first does not, by itself, prove it caused what came next.

prompt: Maya says, "Most ninth-grade classrooms asked in a survey liked requiring phone lockers during class. So every person affected will benefit from it." What is the logical gap in Maya's reasoning?

choices.text: It uses a source with no possible incentive.
It moves from what most surveyed people liked to what everyone will benefit from.
It gives too many definitions of the same term.
It includes a specific number instead of a story.

explanation: The logical gap is moving from most surveyed people to every affected person. Liking a plan also does not prove the plan helps.

prompt: A report says the average result improved after sending medication reminder texts before evening doses. Sam concludes, "That means every person in the group improved." What is the logical gap in Sam's reasoning?

choices.text: It uses a source with no possible incentive.
It states the conclusion before the evidence.
It treats an average change as proof that every individual changed the same way.
It gives too many definitions of the same term.

explanation: The logical gap is that an average can go up even when some people do not improve.

prompt: At a planning meeting about four-day workweek test for software engineers, Avery says, "In one group that tried testing a Monday-through-Thursday workweek, staff quitting fell by 15% compared with the four weeks before the test. choices.text: It uses a source with no possible incentive.
It includes a specific number instead of a story.
It states the conclusion before the evidence.
It generalizes from one case to every case without enough support.

explanation: The logical gap is the jump from one case to every case. One success does not prove the same thing will happen everywhere.

It gives too many definitions of the same term.
It states the conclusion before the evidence.
It moves from what most surveyed people liked to what everyone will benefit from.

explanation: The logical gap is moving from most surveyed people to every affected person. Liking a plan also does not prove the plan helps.

prompt: A report says the average result improved after sending overdraft warning texts before fees post. Sam concludes, "That means every person in the group improved." What is the logical gap in Sam's reasoning?

choices.text: It treats an average change as proof that every individual changed the same way.
It uses a source with no possible incentive.
It gives too many definitions of the same term.
It states the conclusion before the evidence.

explanation: The logical gap is that an average can go up even when some people do not improve.

prompt: At a planning meeting about timed-entry passes for weekend park trails, Avery says, "In one group that tried using timed-entry passes for weekend trail visits, trail crowding complaints fell by 33% compared with the four weeks before the test. So this plan will work in every similar setting." What is the logical gap in Avery's reasoning?

choices.text: It includes a specific number instead of a story.
It generalizes from one case to every case without enough support.
It uses a source with no possible incentive.
It gives too many definitions of the same term.

explanation: The logical gap is the jump from one case to every case. One success does not prove the same thing will happen everywhere.

prompt: You are unsure whether extending evening bus service until midnight would reduce late-shift commute complaints. Then you learn about one small four-week test where late-shift commute complaints fell by 22% compared with the four weeks before the test. How should your confidence change?

choices.text: Raise confidence somewhat, while staying open to better evidence.
Lower confidence because any small test counts against the idea.
Keep confidence exactly the same even though the evidence points the right way.
Treat the evidence as proof that the opposite claim is true.

explanation: A small test that points the right way should raise confidence somewhat, but it is not final proof.

prompt: You are unsure whether requiring phone lockers during class would reduce classroom disruption reports. Then you see a random comparison using two similar groups. In the group that used the plan, classroom disruption reports fell by 31% compared with the four weeks before the test. In the group without the plan, the same result did not improve. How should your confidence change?

choices.text: Lower confidence because comparison groups make evidence weaker.
Raise confidence a lot, while still avoiding total certainty.
